name: Fetch
description: The Fetch, also known as a fetch apparition or a coimseadair in Irish folklore, is a supernatural double or apparition of a living person. Its appearance is generally considered an omen, most often of impending death. The fetch is not simply a ghost, but rather a tangible manifestation, sometimes described as a perfect replica of the individual it foretells doom for. Sightings of a person's fetch by themselves are said to be a sign of imminent death, while sightings by others close to the individual are thought to indicate a serious illness or other misfortune. The appearance of one's own fetch is considered the most ominous and inescapable of all possible encounters. Some accounts suggest that the fetch is a separate entity, perhaps a guardian spirit gone awry, rather than a mere reflection of the individual. The belief in the fetch highlights the strong connection between the living and the spirit world in Irish folklore, where the boundaries between reality and the supernatural are often blurred.
subtitle: A death omen apparition.
appearance: The Fetch appears as an exact duplicate of a living person, including clothing, mannerisms, and even their current emotional state. It is often described as pale or spectral, but otherwise indistinguishable from the original individual at first glance.
habitat: Fetches can appear anywhere the person they mirror might be found, but are more commonly reported in places with strong supernatural associations, such as old houses, crossroads, or areas known for paranormal activity. They are not tied to a specific geographic location, but rather to the individual they represent.
behavior: The Fetch does not typically interact directly with others. Its primary function is to appear as a warning sign. It may simply stand silently, observe, or mimic some action of the person it represents. Its appearance is often sudden and unsettling, leaving witnesses with a sense of dread and foreboding. The fetch is not believed to be malicious, but its presence is undeniably a harbinger of misfortune.

keyMyths.title: The Farmer and His Fetch
keyMyths.description: A farmer returning home late one night encountered his own double walking towards him. Stricken with fear, he rushed home and fell ill. Despite the best efforts of the village healer, he died within the week. This tale highlights the belief that seeing one's own fetch is a direct premonition of death.
keyMyths.source: Briggs, K. M. (1976). A Dictionary of Fairies.

keyMyths.title: The Fetch of the Sick Child
keyMyths.description: A mother awoke one morning to find her child's fetch sitting at the foot of his bed. Distraught, she summoned a priest who performed an exorcism. While the child remained gravely ill, he eventually recovered, suggesting that intervention might avert the worst outcome when a fetch is witnessed by others.
keyMyths.source: Lysaght, P. (1986). The Banshee: The Irish Death-Messenger.

keyMyths.title: The Unseen Fetch
keyMyths.description: A young woman felt an inexplicable sense of unease for several days. Her friends reported seeing her in places she hadn't been, engaging in activities she hadn't done. Shortly thereafter, she was involved in a serious accident, confirming that the fetch can manifest even when not directly observed by the individual it mirrors.
keyMyths.source: Evans Wentz, W. Y. (1911). The Fairy-Faith in Celtic Countries.

sources.author: Briggs, K. M.
sources.description: A comprehensive guide to fairies and other supernatural beings in folklore, including detailed entries on the Fetch.

sources.description: A classic anthropological study of fairy beliefs in Celtic regions, offering valuable insights into the cultural context of the Fetch.

sources.description: A collection of firsthand accounts of supernatural encounters in the West of Ireland, including stories about fetches.
